title: `uname -m` in an arm32 container on an arm64 host reports it as `aarch64`
body:
### Issue Description

I noticed that `uname -m` behaves differently in an arm32 container depending on whether it runs on an arm64 host. On arm64, it reports the machine as `aarch64`, whereas on other hosts it reports `armv7l` or something like that.

It seems the proper fix is to start a new shell inside the container with `setarch linux32` command to run all subprocesses in a new environment where the machine is presented as 32-bit. However, identifying the issue and finding this solution took quite a long time. So, even though it's fixable on the user side, I don't think the situation is ideal.

`setarch linux32` calls the personality(2) system call with `PER_LINUX32` to alter uname’s output.

My question is: shouldn't podman invoke the same system call automatically, so users don't need to worry about discrepancies in uname’s output for that special case of arm32/arm64 guest/host case?

At the moment, host information leaks into the container in an undesirable way.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Run `podman run --platform linux/arm --rm debian uname -m` on an arm64 machine
2. See its output


### Describe the results you received

I expected `armv7l` or something similar

### Describe the results you expected

I got `aarch64`

### podman info output

```yaml
N/A
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: would setting the value of `--personality` help in this case?

It looks like it does, but that's another extra command-line flag that always has to be set for arm32/arm64 guest/host combinations to work appropriately, so it has the same discoverability issue as `setarch linux32`. Can we enable `--personality=LINUX32` by default when the container is 32-bit and the host is 64-bit?

> Can we enable --personality=LINUX32 by default when the container is 32-bit and the host is 64-bit?

The OS/arch reported by the image metadata has been historically unreliably so we don't really use it. I don't think it i sup to podman to set this and who knows if this breaks anyone who would rely on the current behavior.
